trajectory.description: Observation: Starting from scratch on ASVspoof 2021 LA deepfake detection. Need to establish a fast, working baseline in iteration 0 to validate the pipeline and measure iteration time. Analysis: Using microsoft/wavlm-large (pre-downloaded, 317M params) as the encoder with a simple mean+std pooling classifier gives a strong starting point. To keep the first iteration fast, I will freeze the encoder and train only the classifier head for 2 epochs. This should complete in ~20-30 minutes while still yielding a reasonable baseline EER. Decision: Write model.py, train.py, predict.py, and a small mock-test helper. Run training with --freeze_encoder, then test predict.py locally on mock data, and immediately submit_eval to measure end-to-end iteration time.

trajectory.description: Observation: Previous run failed because the raw audio attention_mask [B, 141936] was multiplied with encoder hidden states [B, 443, H] in the pooling layer, causing a dimension mismatch. Analysis: WavLM's CNN feature extractor downsamples raw audio by ~320x, so the time dimension in hidden states is much smaller. We need to either compute the frame-level mask from raw lengths or avoid masking altogether. Decision: Implement exact frame-length computation using the encoder's conv_kernel/conv_stride config in model.py, and use that to create a proper frame mask for mean+std pooling. Rewrite model.py, train.py, predict.py cleanly. Then run 1 epoch with frozen encoder to validate the full pipeline, test predict.py locally on mock data, and immediately submit_eval to measure iteration time.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 2 failed at the dev evaluation stage because torch.sigmoid(logits).cpu().numpy() was called on a BFloat16 tensor, which NumPy does not support. Training itself completed successfully in ~40 min with batch_size=32. Analysis: The fix is to cast to float32 before moving to CPU/NumPy. To accelerate the baseline, I will increase batch_size to 64 and keep the encoder frozen for this fast validation run. Decision: Rewrite train.py with the BFloat16->float32 fix, add a few throughput optimizations (num_workers=8, TF32), train 1 epoch with --freeze_encoder, test predict.py on the mock test set, and immediately submit_eval to get the first EER measurement and end-to-end iteration time.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 3 finished training successfully: 1 epoch with frozen encoder, batch_size=64, train loss 0.3791, dev EER 3.62%. The pipeline is stable but no eval has been submitted yet. Analysis: Before scaling training, I must confirm that predict.py loads the checkpoint correctly and that the eval workflow is functional. The exact checkpoint save format in train.py is not fully visible, so predict.py should handle both dict-wrapped and raw state_dict formats. Decision: Update predict.py with robust checkpoint loading, inspect the saved checkpoint keys, run a quick local inference on mock_test, and immediately submit_eval to obtain the first hidden-test EER baseline and measure eval latency.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 4 failed because torch.load defaulted to weights_only=True in PyTorch 2.6, and our existing checkpoint contains a numpy scalar (best_eer from sklearn roc_curve). Analysis: We cannot change the saved checkpoint, but we can load it safely with weights_only=False. The training pipeline itself is healthy (1 epoch frozen encoder gave 3.62% dev EER). Decision: Fix predict.py to use weights_only=False, also fix train.py to cast numpy scalars to Python float before saving (for future checkpoints). Run predict.py on the local mock_test to verify end-to-end inference works, then immediately submit_eval with the existing best.pt to get the first hidden-test EER baseline.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 5 eval failed with a soundfile crash (seek/read error on a FLAC file in the hidden test set). The mock local test succeeded, so the issue is test-set-specific audio decoding. Analysis: soundfile/libsndfile can fail on certain FLAC variants. torchaudio (pre-installed) uses a different backend and can often read files that soundfile cannot. Adding a torchaudio fallback in predict.py should make inference robust. Decision: Rewrite predict.py with torchaudio fallback for both duration probing and audio loading. Run local mock test, then immediately submit_eval with the existing best.pt checkpoint to finally get a baseline EER and validate the end-to-end pipeline.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 6 achieved a baseline EER of 17.92% on the hidden test set using a frozen WavLM-large encoder with mean+std pooling trained for 1 epoch. Dev EER was only 3.62%, revealing a massive generalization gap caused by unseen test codecs (telephony degradation) and unseen attacks. Analysis: To improve out-of-domain generalization without blowing up training time, we should (1) add audio augmentations that simulate telephone bandwidth and noise, (2) switch to attentive pooling concatenated with mean pooling for richer sequence aggregation, and (3) use weighted sampling to balance the highly imbalanced classes during training. Keeping the encoder frozen keeps epochs fast (~40 min each), allowing us to train for 3 epochs within budget. Decision: Rewrite model.py with attentive+mean pooling and train.py with on-the-fly augmentations (telephony resampling, additive noise, random gain) plus WeightedRandomSampler. Train for 3 epochs with frozen encoder, test locally, then submit_eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 7 completed 3 epochs of frozen-encoder training with telephone augmentation, attentive+mean pooling, and weighted sampling. Dev EER improved from 3.62% to 0.87%, but no hidden-test eval was submitted, so the actual generalization improvement is unknown. The training curves for iter_7 are empty, suggesting metrics.jsonl was not written in the expected format. Analysis: Before committing to longer fine-tuning runs, I must measure the hidden-test EER for this checkpoint. I also need to fix metrics logging so future curves are populated. Decision: Submit eval immediately for checkpoints/best.pt. Simultaneously rewrite train.py to guarantee per-step metrics are flushed to logs/iter_{RAB_ITERATION}/metrics.jsonl in the exact JSON schema required by the harness.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 8 achieved a new best test EER of 12.41% (down from 17.92%) by adding telephone augmentations, attentive+mean pooling, and weighted sampling while keeping the encoder frozen. The training curves were empty because metrics.jsonl logging was missing. There remains a massive generalization gap (dev EER 0.87% vs test 12.41%), suggesting frozen WavLM features are not adapting to the unseen telephony codecs in the hidden test set. Analysis: The biggest remaining lever is fine-tuning the encoder (or at least the transformer layers) so representations adapt to codec-degraded speech. To preserve low-level stability, I will freeze only the CNN feature extractor and fine-tune the transformer + projection with a small LR (2e-5), while keeping a larger LR (1e-3) for the classifier head. I will also merge train+dev for more data, add explicit mu-law/a-law companding augmentations to simulate telephony codecs, and fix per-step metrics logging. Decision: Backup the previous best checkpoint, write a new train.py that implements these changes, run 2 epochs, verify predict.py on mock_test, and submit_eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 9 OOM'd when fine-tuning WavLM-large transformer with batch_size=32. The attention activations for 24 layers x 16 heads x ~750 frames x batch 32 exceed 31GB. Analysis: Full fine-tuning requires either gradient checkpointing (trades compute for memory) or a much smaller batch size. With gradient checkpointing + batch_size=16, the encoder recomputes activations during backward instead of storing them, fitting within 31GB. To further stabilize, I'll freeze the CNN feature extractor and the first 8 transformer layers, training only the top 16 layers with a small LR (1e-5). This also speeds up training. Decision: Rewrite train.py with gradient checkpointing, layer-wise freezing, batch_size=16, and keep all prior augmentations + combined train/dev. Train for 2 epochs. If this run completes, submit_eval.
